title: Iran Oil Returns, Fed Holds Hawkish, Asia Defends Currencies
subhead: Iran has exported over 40 million barrels since sanctions relief began, Brent crude has fallen below $72, and the world's major central banks are converging on hawkish policy even as energy prices retreat — while Asia's currency interventions expose the unequal burden of the Iran war's aftermath.

sections.heading: Iran Oil Floods Market as Sanctions Relief Kicks In
sections.body:
Iran has exported more than 40 million barrels of crude oil since the US lifted its naval blockade, according to parliament speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, with tanker-tracking firm TankerTrackers.com estimating the figure closer to 50 million barrels. Iranian crude is now selling at roughly a 20% premium to pre-war levels, Ghalibaf claimed, a reversal from the $10–15/bbl discount Tehran previously offered to offset sanction risk.

The US Treasury's General License X, issued under the June 17 MOU, waived sanctions on Iranian oil production, delivery, and sale through August 21 — the end of the 60-day negotiation window. According to the Atlantic Council, Iran was projected to export just over 720,000 bpd in June, down from a pre-war average of roughly 1.5–1.8 million bpd between November 2025 and April 2026, reflecting war-damaged infrastructure and continued Strait of Hormuz disruptions.

China, historically Iran's largest buyer, may have limited appetite for increased purchases — it stockpiled discounted crude throughout 2025 and Iranian supply now accounts for only about 11% of Chinese imports (down from 14% in 2025), while Russia, Saudi Arabia, and Brazil each supply more. India, running dangerously low on fuel after months of supply disruptions, could emerge as a more promising destination, the Atlantic Council analysis notes.
sections.whyItMatters: The sanctions waiver has unlocked a substantial volume of Iranian crude, but the premium pricing and infrastructure damage mean Iran is not simply returning to pre-war export levels. The reallocation of supply — with India potentially replacing some Chinese demand — reshapes Asian energy trade flows.

sections.heading: Brent Below $72 as Hormuz Reopening Gains Traction
sections.body:
Brent crude fell to $71.60/bbl on July 5, down 24% over the past month and nearly 40% from the war's peak of $118/bbl in April, according to Trading Economics. The plunge reflects a combination of Iranian supply returning, the Strait of Hormuz reopening under a 60-day toll-free transit arrangement, and weakening demand signals.

OPEC cut its 2026 oil demand growth forecast for the second time, to 970,000 bpd — about 200,000 bpd below its previous estimate, according to The National. The IEA's May report was even starker, forecasting global demand contracting by 420 kb/d year-on-year in 2026, about 1.3 mb/d below pre-war forecasts.

Spot premiums for Asian crude have fallen back to pre-war levels, though insurers and shippers remain cautious. Mine-clearing operations in the Strait of Hormuz could take 40–50 days before full shipping traffic normalizes, and Iran's recent strikes on commercial vessels during the brief post-MOU breakdown in late June underscored the ongoing risk.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil's rapid retreat masks a fragile equilibrium: demand is falling alongside supply recovery, and the physical infrastructure of Gulf shipping remains impaired. The 40–50 day demining timeline means full normalization is weeks away, and Iran's insistence on retaining administrative control over Hormuz adds uncertainty.

sections.heading: Fed's Warsh Signals No Rate Cuts, ECB Weighs July Hike
sections.body:
New Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, speaking at the ECB Forum in Sintra on July 1, declined to hint at a July rate decision but said inflation remains 'too high' and pledged to deliver the 2% target. The Fed's preferred PCE gauge showed core inflation at 3.4% in May, with headline at 4.1% — both far above target. Warsh said anyone expecting comfort with above-2% inflation 'would be disappointed' and announced five task forces to overhaul the Fed's data and policy approach.

Across the Atlantic, ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the central bank will remain 'proactive' against high inflation despite the Iran deal easing energy prices, according to Bloomberg and Reuters. ECB Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch kept a July rate hike 'in play,' noting that while the Iran deal eases near-term energy pressure, pipeline inflation effects from the conflict have yet to fully manifest, Global Banking and Finance reported.

Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey flagged rising leverage in government bond and equity markets — including hedge fund and ETF leverage — as a financial stability risk, along with the divergence between bond yields and equity valuations.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal has removed one source of inflation pressure, but major central banks are not easing — they're tightening or holding firm. Warsh's hawkish debut, the ECB's potential July hike, and the BOJ's recent move to 1% all signal that the inflation aftermath of the Iran war is far from absorbed, even as energy prices retreat.

sections.heading: BOJ Raises Rates to 31-Year High, Yen Still at 160
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1% — the highest level since 1995 — at its June 16 meeting, continuing the normalization of a policy framework that kept rates negative for years. But the yen barely budged, trading around 160 against the dollar, as Japan deployed over 11.7 trillion yen ($72.8 billion) in foreign-exchange intervention from April to May with only fleeting effect.

According to CNBC, the structural drivers of yen weakness remain firmly in place: a 180+ basis point gap between 10-year JGB yields (2.64%) and US Treasuries (4.45%) keeps the carry trade attractive. BOJ board member Toichiro Asada cast the sole dissenting vote against the hike, reflecting Prime Minister Takaichi's reflationary leanings — she appointed two dovish academics to the board earlier this year, including Asada.

At the G7 summit, Takaichi pushed a joint critical minerals stockpiling initiative to counter China's grip on supply chains, positioning Japan as a strategic actor in resource security even as its currency policy struggles gain traction.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is caught in a policy bind: it needs higher rates to defend the yen and fight import-driven inflation, but the Takaichi administration favors reflation and the rate gap with the US makes intervention expensive and largely ineffective. The $72.8 billion already spent on FX intervention with minimal lasting impact illustrates the limits of unilateral currency defense.

sections.heading: Trump Escalates Digital Tax Tariff Threat to All Countries
sections.body:
President Trump on June 26 escalated his fight over digital services taxes, threatening a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes such a levy on American technology companies, Reuters and CNBC reported. The threat extends far beyond the initial salvo at France — it now covers every nation with a DST, including the UK, Italy, Austria, Spain, Turkey, and India.

The original dispute centers on France's 3% digital services tax, imposed in 2019 on revenues earned by tech firms including Amazon, Apple, Google, and Meta within French borders. Trump had earlier threatened 100% tariffs specifically on French wine and champagne. French wine and spirits exports to the US already face a 15% tariff (up from 10%), and FEVS, the French wine exporters' federation, said exports to the US fell 21% last year. French President Macron declared that 'the US does not decide European law,' refusing to back down.

Canada scrapped its own digital services tax last year under US pressure. The EU is separately advancing legislation to tax Big Tech to fund its budget, setting up a wider transatlantic confrontation.
sections.whyItMatters: This is no longer a bilateral France-US dispute — it is a systemic confrontation between US tech policy and sovereign taxation rights. If Trump follows through, the 100% tariff would hit dozens of countries simultaneously, dwarfing previous trade wars in scope and potentially fracturing G7 unity on economic coordination.

sections.heading: EU's 21st Russia Sanctions Package and Oil Cap Freeze
sections.body:
The European Commission announced its 21st sanctions package against Russia on June 9, targeting energy, financial services, and trade. Crucially, the package freezes the oil price cap adjustment mechanism until January 2027, citing the need for market stability during the Iran war's disruption of Gulf supply routes, Baker McKenzie reported.

The package adds 30 more vessels to the shadow fleet blacklist (now over 600 total), and for the first time targets vessels providing bunkering and support services to the shadow fleet. It extends transaction bans to 31 additional Russian banks and targets 20 entities in third countries, including banks, crypto platforms, and oil traders facilitating sanctions circumvention. A potential full ban on crypto-asset services from third countries used to evade sanctions is also proposed.

The UK separately announced its own sanctions package targeting Russia's shadow fleet and financial networks, with British troops seizing a vessel in the English Channel. Canada added 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, and defense-industrial sector.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU's decision to freeze the oil price cap adjustment — rather than tighten it — is a pragmatic concession to the Iran war's supply shock, but it effectively gives Russia a sanctions reprieve on its most important revenue stream. Meanwhile, the expansion of sanctions to crypto platforms and shadow fleet services signals an escalation in financial warfare tools.

sections.heading: Asia's Currency Defense: Intervention Meets Structural Headwinds
sections.body:
Energy-importing Asian economies are fighting a losing battle against currency depreciation. The Korean won fell to its lowest since the global financial crisis, the Indonesian rupiah breached 18,000/dollar, and the Indian rupee and Philippine peso hit record lows, Bloomberg reports via BusinessWorld.

South Korea's finance ministry said it will step up oversight of offshore currency derivatives. The Philippines asked banks to limit non-deliverable forward contracts to 'economic purposes.' India tightened banks' net open position limits to $100 million. Indonesia unexpectedly raised interest rates and said Bank Indonesia is active in currency markets 'around the world, around the clock.' India's RBI has built a short-dollar book of roughly $115 billion, including offshore derivative positions.

Analysts at MUFG note that curbing offshore derivatives may ease pressure but cannot reverse the trend without a shift in fundamentals — which means lower energy prices. As Bank Indonesia's aggressive rate hike demonstrated, monetary policy alone cannot offset the terms-of-trade shock from the Iran war.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war's energy shock is being transmitted to emerging markets through currency channels, draining reserves and forcing interest rate hikes in economies that can least afford them. India's $115 billion short-dollar position and Japan's $72.8 billion in FX intervention represent enormous fiscal costs with diminishing returns — a pattern that will persist until energy prices normalize fully.

bottomLine: The post-Iran-war financial landscape is taking shape: oil prices have cratered below $72 as Tehran's crude re-enters the market, but the inflationary damage is far from absorbed — the Fed, ECB, and BOJ are all holding hawkish or tightening, with Warsh explicitly ruling out comfort above 2%. Asia's energy-importing economies are bleeding reserves to defend currencies that structural rate gaps and war-driven terms-of-trade shocks keep pressuring. Trump's blanket 100% tariff threat against any country with a digital services tax opens a new front in global trade conflict that could dwarf previous disputes. And the EU's decision to freeze the Russia oil price cap adjustment — even as it expands shadow fleet and crypto sanctions — illustrates the awkward trade-off between squeezing Moscow and keeping energy markets stable during a Gulf crisis. The Iran deal removed the acute shock; it did not remove the aftershocks.
sources: CNBC, Bloomberg, Reuters, Al Jazeera, Atlantic Council, Trading Economics, Baker McKenzie, BusinessWorld/Bloomberg, France24, Euractiv, Le Monde, IEA, OPEC via The National, Elysée/G7 official statement, ECB, Bank of Japan, TankerTrackers.com, FEVS, CNBC ECB Forum coverage, Global Banking and Finance, FXStreet, IEA Short-Term Energy Outlook
